6. Bilinear Forms

6.2. Symmetric forms

  • Suppose we are given a symmetric/Hermitian form over a finite-dimensional real/complex space.

  • Def An orthogonal basis of satisfies for all .

    • Prop is nondegenerate if and only if for all , i.e. has nonzero diagonal entries.

  • Def The orthogonal space to a subspace of is

    (50)

  • Def is nondegenerate if i.e. for all nonzero , there exists such that . is nondegenerate if and only if is invertible.

    • Def Suppose is a subspace of . A form on is nondegenerate on if its restriction to is a nondegenerate form.
    • Ex A form may be degenerate on a subspace, though it is nondegenerate on the whole space and vice versa. For example, consider

    (51)

    • Extend the kernel of a degenerate form to a basis of . Then the matrix of with respect to this basis is , so it remains to classify nondegenerate bilinear forms.

  • Lem 1. Suppose is a subspace of . The following statements are equivalent:

    1. A symmetric form is nondegenerate on .
    2. .

    Proof. (1) (2) is easy. We cannot use orthogonal projection to prove (3) because we not know if an orthogonal basis exists (proved below). Instead consider applying a change of basis to , and use the fact that remains symmetric.

  • Lem 2. Let be a nondegenerate symmetric or Hermitian form on , and let . If for all , then .

  • Lem 3. If a symmetric/Hermitian form is not identically zero, there is a vector in such that .

    Proof. For a symmetric form, , so if the LHS is nonzero one of the vectors on the RHS must be nonzero. For a Hermitian form, replace by to make real first.

  • Thm Every symmetric bilinear form, degenerate or nondegenerate, admits an orthogonal basis.

    Proof.
    Suppose is identically zero. Then every bases is orthogonal.

    Suppose is not identically zero. Proceed by induction on . is trivially true. By Lem 3 there exists a vector such that . Let . The form is clearly nondegenerate on , therefore by Lem 1. Apply the induction hypothesis to .

  • Cor For nondegenerate symmetric forms we can apply orthogonal projection.

    • [] Prop Orthogonal projection is a symmetric operator.
  • Cor A symmetric form on a f.d. vector space admits an orthogonal basis in which is a diagonal matrix with 1s, −1s, and 0s on the diagonal.

  • Def Let be a real vector space. A symmetric form on is:

    • Positive definite if for all nonzero .
    • Positive semidefinite if for all nonzero .
    • Indefinite if not is not positive definite.

  • Prop (Sylvester Criterion) is positive definite if and only if the submatrices (called corner matrices) have positive determinants for all .

    • The same result does not hold for checking for positive semidefiniteness. We instead need to verify all principle minors of are nonnegative, not just the leading ones.

6.3. Hermitian forms

  • Def Let be a vector space over .

    • A semilinear form satisfies and .
    • A sesquilinear form is semilinear in the first splot and linear in the second slot.

    • A Hermitian form is a sesquilinear form satisfying Hermiticity: .

      • Prop equals its conjugate transpose .
      • Prop The space of Hermitian forms forms an dimensional real vector space (subtle!).
    • Def (Inner product space) A Euclidean/Hermitian space is a real/complex vector space equipped with a positive definite symmetric/Hermitian form.
  • Prop Everything about symmetric operators from last section carries over for Hermitian forms.
  • [TODO] Complexification of a real vector space
  • Prop Let be a linear operator whose matrix is a real symmetric matrix. Then

6.4. Signature of a symmetric form

  • Recall by existence of orthogonal basis for symmetric/Hermitian forms, we could obtain a diagonal matrix for a form with 1s, −1s, and some 0s.
  • Def The signature of a nondegenerate form is the tuple .

  • Thm (Sylvester) When , The signature does not depend on choice of basis.

    Proof. Suppose has two orthogonal bases with signatures and such that with . Let be spanned by the basis and be spanned by the basis. Note that since we cannot have a vector such that is both positive and negative. Thus is a subspace of , but the left hand side has dimension greater than , yielding a contradiction. Thus we must have .

    • Cor There are equivalence classes of nondegenerate symmetric forms in an -dimensional vector space.
  • Ex For other fields, the signature is not necessarily invariant under change of basis! Consider and . The quadratic forms and are related by the change of basis and . Thus the parity of is conserved.

6.5. Quadratic forms

  • Def A quadratic form on is a function such that

    1. is even: .
    2. is a (manifestly symmetric) bilinear form on .

  • Prop

    2. There is a bijection between quadratic forms and symmetric bilinear forms, in particular (Consider )
    3. .
  • Ex The quadratic form corresponding to the standard inner product of is .

6.6. Skew-symmetric forms

  • Lem If is skew symmetric and then and are linearly independent.
  • Thm Every nondegenerate skew-symmetric form admits a basis called the sympletic basis such that is a block diagonal matrix with as the blocks.

6.7. Adjoint and Normal Operators

  • Let be a Euclidean or Hermitian space, and be a linear operator.

  • Prop (Riesz representation theorem)
    There exists a unique operator (called the adjoint) such that

    (52)

    Proof. There exists a basis where . Work under this basis. Then

    (53)

    since and commute. Thus for a Euclidean space, and for a Hermitian space. To prove uniqueness, use Lem 2 from 6.2.

  • Def A matrix is self-adjoint or Hermitian if .

    • Prop The eigenvalues of a self-adjoint matrix are real.
      Proof. For an eigenvector , , implying since is nonzero.

    • Cor The eigenvalues of a real symmetric matrix are real.

  • Def is normal if .

    • Ex Self-adjoint operators are normal.

    • Lem If is normal, then

      1. for all .
      2. implies .
      3. Eigenvectors of corresponding to distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal.

6.8. Spectral Theorem

  • [] Lem 1. Suppose is a symmetric matrix. Then has an eigenvalue.

    Proof. Take any nonzero , and consider the list . This is a list of vectors, so it must be linearly dependent, i.e. there exist such that

    (54)

    Factoring the equation and rearranging yields

    (55)

    Each quadratic in the left product corresponds to two conjugate complex roots (), and each term in the right corresponds to a real root. Each quadratic must be injective (prove it), so we must have at least one term in the right product that is not injective. Thus has an eigenvalue.

  • Thm (Real spectral theorem) Suppose is a f.d. Euclidean space, and let be a symmetric operator. Then admits an orthonormal eigenbasis.

    Proof. Take an eigenvector corresponding to eigenvalue (guaranteed to exist by Lem 1), and let . Since , we have the matrix of with respect to the basis is

    (56)

    Suppose . . Thus is invariant under , and the asterisk region is zero. Alternatively we could have taken an orthonormal change of basis and used the fact that . Apply the induction hypothesis to . □

  • Let be a f.d. Hermitian space, and let be a family of self-adjoint operators that mutually commute with each other: .

  • Thm (Commuting observables in QM) The family admits a common orthonormal eigenbasis.

    Proof. Induction on . is trivial. Take eigenvector of in the eigenspace . Then , implying is invariant under each .

    Each has an common orthonormal eigenbasis by the inductive hypothesis. Stitch them together, and we’re done (We are guaranteed that are orthogonal). Edge case: If all are scalar opeartors, then any orthonormal basis works. So we picked above with a nontrivial orthogonal decomposition. □

  • Lem 2. Suppose is a Hermitian space. If is -invariant, then is invariant.

  • Thm (Complex Spectral Theorem) Suppose is a f.d. Hermitian space, and let be a normal operator. Then admits an orthonormal eigenbasis.

    Proof 1. Induction on . is trivial. Take any eigenvalue . Then . Since and commute, is invariant under , is invariant under by Lem 2. Apply the inductive hypothesis.

    Proof 2. Let and . and commute and are both self-adjoint, so we have a common orthonormal eigenbasis. Furthermore, so the eigenvalues of are in the form . □

    Cor Every unitary operator has an orthonormal eigenbasis with eigenvalues .

    Proof. and . This implies .

6.9. Gram–Schmidt and QR decomposition

  • Prop (Gram-Schmidt) Any basis in a Euclidean/Hermitian space can be made orthonormal.

  • Ex Let and be a positive integratable function on which decays rapidly at (faster than any polynomial, but ). is called a weight function. We have a positive-definite Hermitian form defined by

    (57)

    Let be the basis of monomials. Then Gram-Schmidt yields:

    (58)

  • Prop ( decomposition) Every invertible complex matrix can be uniquely written as where is unitary and is upper triangular with positive diagonal.

    Proof. Take columns and apply G.S. to show existence. Suppose . Then is both unitary and upper triangular, so it must be the identity.

    • Cor If is real, then , so is orthogonal.

6.10. Positive Operators and Square Roots

  • Def An operator is positive semidefinite if for all and is self-adjoint.

    • An example of a non-self-adjoint matrix that is PSD is
  • Def is a square root of if .

  • Prop If is positive semidefinite, then has a unique positive semidefinite square root.

    • Prop The set of matrices that commutes with a diagonal matrix with distinct entries is the set of diagonal matrices.
    • [TODO] How do you prove it for Jordan blocks? I guess you don’t because by definition must be self-adjoint :P.

6.11. Polar decomposition

  • Thm (Polar Decomposition)
    Every invertible complex matrix can be uniquely written as where is unitary and is positive. In particular, .

    • If is not invertible, is self-adjoint but not necessarily positive, and there are multiple allowed values for , in particular, is uniquely defined on the orthogonal complement of , and can be extended nonuniquely to the entier space to remain unitary.
    • Geometrically, the polar composition can be interpretted as a rotation plus a scaling along orthogonal axes.

  • Cor (Existence of SVD for matrices)
    Every invertible complex matrix can be written as where and are unitary and is diagonal with positive entries.
    Proof. . Let and .
